summary: This post demonstrates how to model message queues and pub-sub systems in TLA+, providing practical examples that go beyond the typical consensus algorithm tutorials. Hillel walks through implementing an SQS-like queue with writers and readers, then extends it to handle at-least-once delivery semantics with message duplication and retry logic. He then shows a pub-sub variant where each reader has their own queue, including subscription management. The post emphasizes that TLA+ is useful for everyday distributed systems problems, not just verifying Paxos, and calls for more accessible learning resources.
key_points: TLA+ lacks simple, practical examples which makes it harder to learn and harder for people to see its relevance to their actual problems
At-least-once delivery can be modeled three different ways depending on whether you're specifying the protocol itself or a system using the protocol
Type invariants in TLA+ serve double duty as both spec correctness checks and model constraint validation, which should ideally be separated
Function sets and function comprehensions are powerful TLA+ features that elegantly model per-reader queues in pub-sub systems
The either/or construct naturally models nondeterministic failures like dropped messages, failed acks, or message duplication
money_quotes: My problem isn't verifying Paxos, it's figuring out why my microservices aren't deduping the message queue.
If you looked at the most common public examples, you'd probably come away thinking that people only used it for critical consensus algorithms.
TLA+ is an untyped formalism. Lamport argued that type systems in specifications are often a hindrance.
I could just as easily make types like 'two-digit primes except 11' or 'sequences of messages no reader has seen yet.'
Mixing spec invariants and model constraints is bad practice. You should really separate them out.
Function sets are one of the biggest stumbling blocks for people new to TLA+, but they are amazingly powerful once you get used to them.
It took me only a couple minutes to whip each example up but I've been doing this a while- it's probably a lot harder to 'see' these if you're just starting out.
key_insight: TLA+ is most valuable not for verifying exotic consensus algorithms but for modeling the everyday distributed systems problems engineers actually face, like message queue semantics and deduplication logic.
